pattern_recognition: This problem falls under the category of 'Array Manipulation' and 'Hashing'. The key signal is the need to identify duplicates within an array, which often suggests the use of a hash set or similar data structure for efficient lookups.

approaches.title: Brute Force
approaches.intuition: Check every pair of elements to see if any two are the same. This is straightforward but inefficient.
approaches.algorithm: Iterate through each element and compare it with every other element.

approaches.title: Optimal Approach
approaches.intuition: Utilize a hash set to track seen numbers. If a number is found in the set, it means a duplicate exists.
approaches.algorithm: Iterate through the array, adding each number to a hash set and checking for existence.

dry_run: For the input [1, 2, 3, 1]: Start with an empty set. Check 1, add it to the set. Check 2, add it to the set. Check 3, add it to the set. Check 1 again, it's already in the set, so return true.
edge_cases: An empty input should return false. A single element array should return false. An array with duplicates like [1, 1] should return true. Large inputs should be handled efficiently. Consider integer overflow if using a specific data type.
common_pitfalls: 1. Not considering the case of an empty array (should return false). 2. Using a nested loop without realizing the inefficiency for large inputs. 3. Forgetting to check for duplicates before adding to the set, leading to incorrect results.
